author: 0xunclebill
content:
I think we should be a lil more strategic with future devnets. now that we have more mature Gloas impls I think we should gate participation on passing spec tests. And we should also in general give ourselves more time between spec release and devnet launch so that we have more time to work on passing spec tests. 

Feels like the last few devnets we’ve been kinda scrambling. Trying to think of what we could have done better to catch things earlier and make these devnets feel a lil more productive

author: .paulharris
content: probably depends largely on the root cause...

author: 0xunclebill
content:
I just think launching a devnet 48 hours after spec release is a bit aggressive. And not many people around to debug on weekends also makes it tough. If we launch on a Monday for example we have more people online throughout the week to catch things as they happen

On our end we’re brainstorming ideas to integrate panda cli into our devops flow to catch potential issues in real time so that we can more quickly respond

author: .paulharris
content:
i get what you're saying, just that if its an untested consensus issue it wouldnt have helped at all having spec test gates etc etc, just making the gates higher but not for the right reasons, so its probably worth understanding what happened before deciding what to do about the breakage etc.
I do agree 2 days after a spec release is aggressive, and clearly only had a subset of clients etc. but we want to be careful about making 'rules' if we don't even know if it would have helped prevent the issue.

author: 0xunclebill
content: yeah not trying to to create new rules, just feel like the strategy of spinning up devnets as soon as possible can actually hurt productivity sometimes

So I built a third. That burned down, fell over, then sank into the swamp. But the fourth one stayed up. And that's what you're going to get, Lad, the strongest castle in all of England.

author: barnabasbusa
content:
I'm sus of this flag:`    export MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS_REQUESTS=256` 

Could everyone confirm that its configurable on your node? 

We set this flag so that our nodes wouldn't store 18d worth of random blob data, but this might cause DA issues on nodes that don't take this value into consideration 

This is a flag that we have used before on blob devnet, but we only have lh and prysm on those

author: tersec
content: Nimbus appears to respect it as a runtime config. All the references are to `cfg.MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_DATA_COLUMN_SIDECARS_REQUESTS` where `cfg` is Nimbus's `RuntimeConfig` type

author: barnabasbusa
content: we gonna do a quick relaunch with as many default mainnet variables as possible. And limit our blob throughput to about 3-5 blobs per block.
